speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. October 10, 1995. Survivor, Paula Tencer, born Altura. The interviewer is Dina Cohen. The interview is taking place in Fort Lee, New Jersey, USA. The interview will be in English. My name is Dina Cohen. Today is October 10, 1995. I am interviewing Paula Tencer, born Altura, in Fort Lee, New Jersey, USA. The interview will be in English. Would you please state your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Tencer, P-A-U-L-A T-E-N-C-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Altura, A-L-T-U-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an eastern part of Poland in a small tow-- town, Brody. Brody, strategically was a-- played a very important part during World War II because it was a direct unobstructed passageway to and from Russia. So soldiers, troops going and re-- and retreating had to pass our town. Brody had a population of 22,000. It was predominantly Jewish. 18,000 were Jewish. The rest were Ukrainians and Poles. Brody was a pleasant town. Everything was within close range. Everything was on a smaller scale, but we had everything-- movie theater, schools, municipal structures, everything was-- We had a marketplace in the center of our town. It was a white stone arcade where the farmers with-- with horse-driven wagons would bring in their livestock and produce to sell. And in that arcade there were stores-- individual stores-- where they bought whatever they needed. The-- the more traveled streets were asphalt, but the other side streets were stone paved. We had two-story buildings, some with balconies. And at the street level, there was stores. There-- we had synagogues within every neighborhood. We also had a very impressive temple, which was famous through Eastern Europe because many famous cantors originated from our town, and rabbis. The-- you walked into that temple so you felt its splendor and spirituality. There was a cantor and a choir and the acoustics were great. Sometimes my father took us on certain occasions. But most of the time, he prayed at synagogue near us. It was-- the name of it was Beth El [? Amos. ?] Now the-- we had a main street. We called it Zlota Uliczka, which was Golden Street. Girls promenaded there, flirting with the boys. The boys were flirting with them, and they pretended they don't see it. We had a park with chestnut trees-- lined with chestnut trees and benches. And in the summer, there was a podium where military band played and the local girls danced with the soldiers. It was fun for us children. And Brody was surrounded by forests. Within walking distance, you were at the edge of town. And passing a field, you found yourself in a forest-- pine tree forest-- which reached four kilometers down. I remember we children, we would go pick berries-- wild strawberries, mushrooms. And then when we had our jugs filled with it, the forester sometimes chased us because we needed a permit. Once I ran and lost a shoe. But I grabbed my shoe and got away, which was fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had a very vibrant and wholesome youth, Jewish youth because it was predominantly Jewish. They played soccer, football. They belonged to a [? [SPEAKING POLISH] ?] which is a health club where they built up their body and exercised. There were Zionist organizations-- all, many activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a private school. I attended a private school. And then I transferred to a public school, co-ed. Then I enrolled to a high school but it was-- of course, it was interrupted due to the war. My father ran a custom-made tailor shop. He employed several tailors. If you wished, you could order the material from us. He cut it, designed it, and the tailors finished it. I think that my father was a very wonderful man-- caring, loving husband and father. Everything interested him. He loved politics. He had a knowledge of the Torah. He liked to discuss passages from it. And he was respected in the synagogue. My father-- I was one of five children. My oldest sister, Laura-- six years older-- was killed. Then came my brother, Max. In Poland, at home, we called him [? Munio. ?] He was three years older than I. He survived. Then was my other half, my brother Zigmund, Ziggy. He was killed. And then my younger sister Rosa, a beautiful little girl. She was 14 at the time. She was killed. Out of a family of seven, two survived. I had aunts and uncles and cousins. I counted 30 people that were killed. [PAUSES] In the '30s the propaganda of Nazis was spread all through Europe. But we lived in Poland, which was territorially removed. It was far from us even though we felt concern for our brethren there in Germany, but we felt it wouldn't touch us.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father had two sisters in Leipzig, Germany. Their-- all their possessions were confiscated and they fled to Poland. After they gained their composure, they told us of their persecution-- how people were being beaten in the streets. Everything was taken away. They had no social life, no school. There were no concentration camps at that time, but we could not believe that such horrendous thing will take place because Germans-- a proud nation who has philosophers, poets, composers, scientists-- how is it possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable that they would design and build gas chambers in 20th century with world looking on. We could not believe that such a thing would happen. Perhaps they'll deprive us of material things. They put us to work. We had no territorial demands. So we would have accepted the simplest life-- just to be in the sun, sleep without fear, and just to live. But that was not given to us. In 1939, there were rumors that there's going-- there's conflict between Poland and Germany. The Germans issued a-- they-- a-- ultimatum to Poland that they should hand over a strip of territory, of their-- their corridor. They also wanted the-- the city of Danzig. We were sitting in front of the radio listening with apprehension. And then the Germans swooped down with airplanes, bombed the cities, causing chaos and fear and confusion. Our cities were filled with military because-- and the reserves were called in immediately. My father's sister Pearl, her son was called to report. She was very upset, agitated. We all went to send him off. And we saw red and white insignia planes flying above thinking those were our planes, Polish planes. But those were German planes camouflaged with machine gun gunning the people. We made it into a ditch, but there were many people killed. In all that confusion, there was nobody to give orders. And the mothers took their sons home and they were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you continue, can we go back to before 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father built a house the year he married my mother. He supervised every scrap of material that went into it. It was a two-story building. It had six apartments with large windows facing the front and all sides. And we had three apartments upstairs and three apartments downstairs with a few steps going down, but the windows were street level, sunny. We occupied upstairs, the largest part. We had 5 tenants. The same-- the same care he applied to the garden. It was a large piece of land. We had cherry trees, peach trees, plum trees, flowers growing. We had summer-- trees that were summer-- apples for summer. And then for the winter, which you put them-- we put them in the basement and they ripened. We had Salzburg-- from Salzburg-- imported plants, pears, which were which were-- the fragrance and the flavor was special. Only some friends had the privilege of getting a basket in the winter. We put them in the cellar on shelves and they ripened. In the garden we had a little house that was-- in the summer, my mother would cook there, which would not overheat our apartment. And we ate in the garden under the trees. There was table. Our tenants had the privilege of user-- using the facilities also. There was a large courtyard with tool shacks. Everybody had it and a lock where they got storage for the winter-- coal and wood. We did not have central heat. We children-- no. My-- my father was-- everything interested him. He liked to discuss politics. He-- he-- was kind. He played a mandolin. He had a beautiful singing voice. He always sang songs. He was a dreamer. He always hoped that someday we'll sell the house and move to Vienna-- the big city, the romantic city of Vienna. My sister Laura, my older sister, she had the benefit of better times because she took piano lessons, completed her education. During my time, my father suffered for some-- for a few years with an ulcer. And they did not diagnose it correctly, so he grew thin. He couldn't ingest food. Instead of being productive, he was on a lounge, suffering. He was at the brink of death. And then he went to Vienna where my mother had a brother, Jacob. He made arrangement for an appointment with a specialist. And they diagnosed that-- that-- that he had ulcers, which were hazardous in that time, those operations. And they operated him, removing part of his intestines. His inst-- intestines were ulcerated and dried up, like crumpled into pieces. He recuperated at my aunt [? Anne's ?] and uncle's house. He gained weight and [INAUDIBLE]. I went to the train to pick him up. My mother said, he looks 20 years younger. He was rejuvenated. And for that, I always thought because of that miracle that week, our father came back to us, that God will spare us from future tragedy. I was one of five children. My oldest sister, Laura, six years older. My brother [? Munio ?] was two years older. He survived. Then came my other half, my twin brother, older by another-- by half an hour, and my sister, Rosa. Because we had a house and tenants and children-- they had children-- we had a built-in playmates. We did not have to play in the streets because it was like a small oasis. We had a garden. We played hide and seek, and all games, and hopscotch, and swinging, all the things. And life was good. I, being born in Poland, speaking the language, educated in Poland-- this was my country. I didn't know any other. I loved Poland until the war. We had a-- a substantial class of intellectuals-- doctors, lawyers, teachers, pharmacists. They had-- there was a middle class by American standards. They lived graciously. They had nice homes. There was also a poor class because Brody was not an industrial city. And so they couldn't get steady jobs. But the people had dignity as long as the daily requirements were met. They were grateful to God. There were also organizations, committees, charitable organizations that helped them discreetly. [PAUSES] My sister Laura was very idealistic. She joined a Zionist organization when-- before the war. Anti-Semitis-- anti-Semitism spread, and she felt that every nationality has a homeland, and we should build a homeland. She was very active in the organization Hanoar [? Hatzioni. ?] And she took me a few times to that organization, to meetings. And then they formed a group of younger children, younger girls. And I joined. We sang songs. We danced the hora. We were telling about the beauty of Palestine and dreaming and hoping of building that pioneer-- pioneering land. My brother Munio was at the age where he already started dating. He was a willow-- willowy handsome young boy, also loved to sing. All our children loved to sing except my brother Ziggy that didn't carry a tune. W-- whenever we watched American films-- musical films-- he quickly remembered the lyrics and the words and the melody. And we used to sing. Sometimes at night, we all sang and the neighbors-- the windows were open. People passing by would stand-- stay there listening. If we would have known that somebody is listening, we would have been embarrassed. But they told my mother how they enjoyed our singing. He liked Western films, so he had a rope and throw lasso. He caught the c-- the dog every time the dog ran by and never missed. At one time he was sitting in a tree, and I ran by. He caught me on the neck. And I was running, so the rope scratched my neck. I was crying, screaming so he bribed me. He gave me money for the movies. I stopped immediately. My brother Ziggy was handsome. You could see that he's broad shouldered, husky, handsome. If my father wanted to reprimand him, he smiled, disarmed you. My sister Rosa was a beauty. She was beautiful. And she as the baby of the family got special attention. It was wonderful being in a family of five children. Sometimes we fought, but then we made up. It was-- life was good. We didn't have to be rich to be happy. Acquiring a dress, a pair of shoes was an event for the holidays. When my mother bought me a pretty dress, I was praying that the weather should be nice because Passover, usually it's still cool. So if it's cool, I have to wear a coat. They wouldn't see my dress. We enjoyed. We didn't take things for granted. In the summer, even though we had fresh air and a garden, it was in our culture that we have to-- the family has to go to the country for fresh air. So my father would rent a cottage. At B-- It was Ponikva, that village-- in a village. It was with forests all around, cornfields, weed growing. And the cornfields had poppy flowers, red, and corn flowers. And we had to milk everything fresh from the cow. We boiled it because we didn't pasteurize. And we had the daily prod-- the dairy products. And other families spend a summer there. And my mother cooked all those delicious meals. And we ate it picnic style. My father would come out weekends. We would pass the forest and sit at the edge of the road on Friday and wait for him. And we saw him coming on a bicycle. He had a knapsack. That joy when we saw him-- we greeted him. And he had chocolate and all good things for us and for the farmers' children. We had cousins in Lemberg-- Lwow-- which was a congested city. So my cousin came to spend a summer with-- with us. She was a dress designer, very glamorous and beautiful, and a sense of humor. And she livened up our summer with singing and joking. We took walks. And when she was leaving, I had tears because there goes that beauty, that romantic idea-- ideas that I had. So she brought that flair of the big city. And she said, you're such a-- she's such a romantic child. I never forgotten that. We didn't want to drink milk, and my mother wanted us to drink. So for every gl-- glass of milk, she paid us. So every child had a piggy bank where we-- we drank a glass of milk, she gave us money. So by the end of summer, we were loaded. My mother would make preserves st-- from strawberries, from raspberries to bring home for the summer. Those forests were beautiful. It was-- it was good to be alive. In 19-- in 1939, when-- in 1941-- in 1939 when the war broke out, we were sitting in a cellar and bombs and machine gunning. We were afraid-- and artillery. And troops were passing because Brody, as I said, was a passageway to Russia-- tanks and troops.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly it was quiet. We were all in a cellar-- and all our tenants-- for safety. My father looked out and he saw a neighbor coming towards him running, waving his arms. The Russians are here. We anticipated that the Germans are going to march in in 1939. And we were frightened. Where do the Russians come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians-- we did not know that the Russians made a pact with Stalin to set-- to split Poland. Eastern Poland was to be occupied by the Russian troops. And the Western Poland will be occupied by the Germans. We were relieved because the Russians would not harm us. We were all-- went to the main highway to watch. We saw tanks rolling in, trucks loaded with the Russian personnel and soldiers. And we were amazed and impressed by their power, by their machinery. And the Russian soldiers were throwing pieces of sugar to the children, which made us realize that in Russia, sugar is an incentive for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they kept coming and coming. And after a few days, they passed. They were going from there to their destination. Then they settled in. The first things the Russians did is free the communists who-- who were in prison. Because on the Polish regime, there were communists who were spreading propaganda, and they were being arrested. And those communists felt their time has come now. They pointed out who was responsible for their arrests? Who was in the military? Who was wealthy? Who was anti-communist? And the Russians had the registration of the civilians. And everybody received a document with a paragraph. Separate them. This one was rich and this one, separated from-- and also there were many arrests and people disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then [PAUSES] they were allowed to bring their wives and their children, the Russian personnel. There was some merchants who anticipated that the Russians are going to confiscate their businesses. So they hid some of their materials, some stuffs. We had some materials, which people purchased from us. So my father and that-- we had a hou-- little house and a garden-- which I did mention-- where my mother cooked and we ate there so it wouldn't be hot. So my father hid there some materials. And the Russians confiscated everything. And very soon there were bread lines. We had to stand in line for products, sometimes for shoes, for soap, for things. We reached the-- the door and they were out of this, of this of material-- out of staples or whatever. My father had to pay taxes so he-- because the tenants paid us rent and they were behind in rent, he waited for them. It was a few days late. He went to pay taxes. The Russian in charge there screamed at him, you contra-revolutionary. We're going to send you to Siberia if it happens again. My father was angry when he came home. And he pulled down the picture of Stalin, which it was compulsory for everybody to have in house. But we hung it back because we were afraid. We saw Stalin's regime dictatorship. People were afraid. We couldn't speak freely. And so we complained. But in retrospect, we did not realize what's ahead of us. When we were doomed to die under the Germans, we would have traded in for Russia. They did not kill Jews. Now the people who were on the German side, Pol-- on the-- the Poland, which was occupied by the Germans, they were the early-- they were the first to suffer from the hands of the Nazis. Boys were being rounded up, loaded on trucks to-- to work, to camps. They were beaten. So at that time, mainly men were frightened-- were being persecuted. So many man, young men, fled on the Russian side-- left their families and ran. And they-- when they came to Brody, they came to the synagogue to be-- to meet Jewish people. So everybody felt obligated to take home a few people. We had four young men in our house. We put mats on the floor. They slept. My mother cooked and served them. And they told us how frightening it is under German occupation. As they were talking and mentioning names, one-- there were two brothers. They mentioned the name of their family, their names, and my mother cried out. The two boys were children of my mother's cousins. We haven't been in touch for some time. And they grew up and we did not recognize them. So it was a joy and they were more welcome-- even more welcome than that. And they stayed for some time. And they received correspondence from home-- that things are status quo. In the meantime the boys were very lonely, missing their parents. Those were boys who were never away from their home. And with distance of time, that fear experience faded out. And they wanted to go back, not knowing that they're going back to death because we did not know that it's going to be a complete extermination. They felt all the hardship that they're going to endure, they'll take. They'll share the destiny with their family. They want to be home. They didn't want to be in a strange surrounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they don't have to register to return because there was a border. All of the young people who registered to return, mind you they only went back because they missed their family. They cried. Some were husbands who want to-- not going back to German-- to Germany. The Russians said, the Germans are your enemies, and we are your friends. You want to go back to the Germans? You are the enemy of communism. And they rounded them up to send them to Siberia. And they rounded up my mother's cousins and all those people, put them on trucks, loaded them on trucks to take them to Siberia. My mother cried, ran with bread and fruit to hand over to the boys. And the Russian chased her away. Get away from the prisoners. She went home heartbroken. And she kept saying, why did we let them register? Why did they register? Later--
